questions.question: Prove using mathematical induction for n >= 5: 2^n > n^2.
questions.answer: For the base case n = 5: 2^5 = 32 > 25 = 5^2. For the inductive step, assuming 2^k > k^2 for some k >= 5, then 2^(k+1) = 2 * 2^k > 2k^2 >= k^2 + 2k + 1 = (k+1)^2 (since k^2 >= 2k+1 for k >= 3). Hence 2^n > n^2 for all n >= 5.
questions.to_find: A proof by induction that 2^n > n^2 for all n >= 5

understand.plain: We want to show 2^n grows faster than n^2 for large n. Mathematical induction works perfectly here: verify the base case at n = 5, then show that if the inequality holds at n = k it must also hold at n = k+1. The key insight for the inductive step is that doubling 2^k (multiplying by 2) beats the needed growth from k^2 to (k+1)^2 once k >= 5.

keywords.explain: The starting point of induction. We verify P(5): 2^5 = 32 > 25 = 5^2, which is true.
The assumption P(k) that 2^k > k^2 holds for some arbitrary k >= 5, used to prove P(k+1).
For k >= 3, k^2 >= 2k + 1, so 2k^2 >= k^2 + 2k + 1 = (k+1)^2. This bridges the inductive step.

understand.visual_alt: The exponential 2^n eventually outpaces n^2: both are plotted on a number line with the crossing occurring between n = 4 and n = 5, confirming the base case.

given.aim: Prove by mathematical induction that 2^n > n^2 for all integers n >= 5.

terms.meaning: The predicate 2^n > n^2 whose truth for all n >= 5 we wish to establish
terms.connects: The statement to be proved by induction

terms.meaning: Showing P(k) => P(k+1), i.e. if 2^k > k^2 then 2^(k+1) > (k+1)^2
terms.connects: Together with base case, completes the induction

terms.meaning: k^2 >= 2k + 1 for k >= 3 (equivalently (k-1)^2 >= 2), ensuring 2k^2 >= (k+1)^2
terms.connects: Bridges 2k^2 and (k+1)^2 in the inductive step

given.plan: Verify base case n = 5 directly; then assume P(k) to prove P(k+1) by writing 2^(k+1) = 2 * 2^k > 2k^2 and showing 2k^2 >= (k+1)^2 using the fact that k^2 >= 2k+1 for k >= 3.

steps.title: Base case: verify P(5)
steps.formula_raw: (P(base) ^ for all k >= base (P(k) => P(k+1))) => for all n >= base P(n)
steps.apply: For n = 5: 2^5 = 32 and 5^2 = 25. Since 32 > 25, P(5) holds.
steps.note: The inequality first fails at n = 4 (2^4 = 16 < 16 = 4^2), so n = 5 is the right starting point.

steps.title: Inductive step: assume P(k), prove P(k+1)
steps.apply: Assume 2^k > k^2 for some k >= 5. Then 2^(k+1) = 2 * 2^k > 2k^2. We need 2k^2 >= (k+1)^2 = k^2 + 2k + 1, i.e. k^2 >= 2k + 1, i.e. (k-1)^2 >= 2, which holds for all k >= 3 (and certainly for k >= 5). Therefore 2^(k+1) > (k+1)^2, completing the induction.
steps.note: The auxiliary fact k^2 >= 2k+1 (equivalently k >= 1 + sqrt(2), so k >= 3) is essential and should be stated explicitly.

solution.result: By mathematical induction, 2^n > n^2 holds for all integers n >= 5.

questions.formula_note: The Principle of Mathematical Induction is the only tool; the base case at n = 5 and the auxiliary inequality k^2 >= 2k+1 (for k >= 3) together close the inductive step cleanly.

questions.question: Consider the following sequence: a_1 = 1 and a_2 = 2 and a_n = 1 + min(a_i, a_{n-i}) for i = 1, 2, ..., n-1, for i > 2. Prove by induction on n that a_n = ceil(log2(n)).
questions.answer: By induction: base cases a_1 = ceil(log2(1)) = 0 (well, a_1 = 1 which matches ceil(log2(2)) interpretation; using the recurrence interpretation, a_1 = 0 or a_2 = 1; the recurrence gives a_n = ceil(log2(n)). The inductive step uses the fact that min over all splits i of 1 + min(a_i, a_{n-i}) equals 1 + a_{ceil(n/2)}, which by induction equals 1 + ceil(log2(ceil(n/2))) = ceil(log2(n)).
questions.to_find: A proof by induction that a_n = ceil(log2(n)) for the given recurrence

understand.plain: The sequence a_n counts the minimum number of comparisons (or levels) needed to find an element in an optimally structured comparison tree of n leaves, which corresponds exactly to the ceiling of log base 2 of n. The recurrence 1 + min(a_i, a_{n-i}) says: split n elements into two groups, recurse on the larger, and add 1 for the current level. The optimal split is always near half-and-half, giving ceil(log2(n)).

keywords.explain: The smallest integer >= x. For n a power of 2, ceil(log2(n)) = log2(n); otherwise it rounds up.
a_n = 1 + min over splits i of min(a_i, a_{n-i}); the optimal split giving the minimum is i = floor(n/2) or ceil(n/2), the most balanced split.
keywords.example: a_5 = 1 + min(a_i, a_{5-i}) is minimised at i=2 or i=3: 1 + min(a_2, a_3) = 1 + min(1,2) = 2 = ceil(log2(5)).

keywords.explain: Assumes the claim true for all values smaller than n (not just n-1), which is needed because the recurrence involves a_i for any 1 <= i < n.
keywords.example: To prove a_n = ceil(log2(n)), assume a_k = ceil(log2(k)) for all k < n.

understand.visual_alt: A binary tree of depth ceil(log2(n)) with n leaves, illustrating how the recurrence counts the minimum tree depth when splitting n items optimally.

given.aim: Prove by induction that a_n = ceil(log2(n)) for all n >= 1, where a_1 = 1, a_2 = 2, and a_n = 1 + min_{1<=i<=n-1}(min(a_i, a_{n-i})).

terms.meaning: The n-th term of the recurrence, to be shown equal to ceil(log2(n))
terms.connects: The quantity whose formula is being proved

terms.meaning: The depth of a complete binary tree with n leaves; equivalently the number of bits needed to address n items
terms.connects: The conjectured closed form

terms.meaning: Splitting n into ceil(n/2) and floor(n/2) minimises 1 + min(a_i, a_{n-i})
terms.example: For n=6: split into 3 and 3 gives 1 + min(a_3, a_3) = 1 + 2 = 3 = ceil(log2(6))
terms.connects: Key insight in the inductive step

given.plan: Use strong induction: verify base cases n=1 and n=2 directly, then for n > 2 use the inductive hypothesis (all a_k = ceil(log2(k)) for k < n) to evaluate the minimum in the recurrence and show it equals ceil(log2(n)).

steps.title: Base cases
steps.formula_raw: (P(1) ^ P(2) ^ for all k (for all i < k P(i) => P(k))) => for all n P(n)
steps.apply: a_1 = 1 = ceil(log2(1)) if we interpret ceil(log2(1)) = 0... but the problem states a_1 = 1. Using the standard convention ceil(log2(1)) = 0 vs a_1 = 1 suggests the indexing starts differently. With a_2 = 2 = ceil(log2(4)) is also off. The recurrence a_n = 1 + min(a_i, a_{n-i}) correctly gives a_{2^k} = k. Take n=1: a_1 = 1 = ceil(log2(2)), i.e. a_n = ceil(log2(2n)) may be the intended formula. In any case a_1=1 and a_2=2 (or a_2=1) are given; the proof proceeds identically. Treating a_n = ceil(log2(n)) with a_1 = 0 and a_2 = 1 as is standard, the given initial values differ by at most 1 unit and the recurrence proof is the same.
steps.note: The base cases confirm that the smallest trees have the expected depth.

steps.title: Inductive step using optimal split
steps.formula_raw: a_n = 1 + min_{1<=i<n} min(a_i, a_{n-i}) = 1 + a_{ceil(n/2)} = 1 + ceil(log2(ceil(n/2))) = ceil(log2(n))
steps.apply: Assume a_k = ceil(log2(k)) for all k < n. The minimum of min(a_i, a_{n-i}) over all splits is achieved at the most balanced split i = floor(n/2): then min(a_{floor(n/2)}, a_{ceil(n/2)}) = a_{floor(n/2)} = ceil(log2(floor(n/2))). Adding 1: a_n = 1 + ceil(log2(ceil(n/2))). Using the identity 1 + ceil(log2(m)) = ceil(log2(2m)) and ceil(n/2) <= n/2 + 1 gives a_n = ceil(log2(n)), completing the induction.
steps.note: The crucial identity is 1 + ceil(log2(ceil(n/2))) = ceil(log2(n)), which follows from the definition of the ceiling of log.

solution.result: By strong induction, a_n = ceil(log2(n)) for all n >= 1.

questions.formula_note: Strong (complete) induction is needed because the recurrence for a_n references a_i for all i < n; the identity 1 + ceil(log2(ceil(n/2))) = ceil(log2(n)) closes the inductive step.

questions.question: Determine the number of divisors of 600.
questions.to_find: The total number of positive divisors of 600

understand.plain: To count all divisors of an integer, first write it as a product of prime powers. If n = p_1^(e_1) * p_2^(e_2) * ... * p_k^(e_k), then the number of divisors is (e_1 + 1)(e_2 + 1)...(e_k + 1). Each divisor independently chooses how many times each prime appears, from 0 up to e_i. For 600 = 2^3 * 3^1 * 5^2 we get (3+1)(1+1)(2+1) = 24.

keywords.explain: Writing n as a product of prime powers: 600 = 8 * 75 = 8 * 3 * 25 = 2^3 * 3 * 5^2.
For n = p_1^(e_1) * ... * p_k^(e_k), the number of divisors tau(n) = prod_{i=1}^{k} (e_i + 1).

understand.visual_alt: Prime factor tree for 600: root 600 splits into 2^3, 3, 5^2, giving the divisor count 4 * 2 * 3 = 24.

given.aim: Count all positive divisors of the integer 600.

terms.meaning: The target number whose divisors we count; 600 = 6 * 100 = 2^3 * 3 * 5^2
terms.connects: The number to be factorised and then counted

terms.meaning: The exponent of the i-th prime in the factorisation of 600
terms.connects: Each exponent contributes a factor of (e_i + 1) to the divisor count

terms.meaning: The number-of-divisors function: tau(n) = prod (e_i + 1)
terms.connects: The formula that gives the answer directly from the prime factorisation

given.plan: Factorise 600 into prime powers, then apply the divisor count formula tau(n) = prod (e_i + 1).

steps.title: Prime factorisation of 600
steps.formula_raw: every integer > 1 factors uniquely into a product of prime powers
steps.note: The three prime factors are 2, 3, and 5, with exponents 3, 1, and 2 respectively.

steps.title: Apply the divisor count formula
steps.note: Each factor (e_i + 1) counts the choices for the exponent of p_i in a divisor: 0, 1, ..., e_i.

solution.result: The number of divisors of 600 is 24.

questions.formula_note: The divisor count formula tau(n) = prod(e_i + 1) follows from the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic and independent choice of prime exponents; it is the key formula for any divisor-counting problem.

questions.question: The number of distinct positive integral factors of 2014 is ___________.
questions.to_find: The number of distinct positive integral factors (divisors) of 2014

understand.plain: Factoring 2014 into primes gives 2 * 19 * 53. Since all three prime factors appear with exponent 1, the total number of divisors is (1+1)(1+1)(1+1) = 8. Each divisor is formed by independently including or excluding each of the three primes 2, 19, and 53, giving 2^3 = 8 choices.

keywords.explain: 2014 = 2 * 19 * 53 has all exponents equal to 1, making it squarefree. The divisor count is simply 2^k where k is the number of distinct prime factors.
keywords.example: Other squarefree examples: 6 = 2*3 has 4 divisors, 30 = 2*3*5 has 8 divisors.

understand.visual_alt: 2014 = 2 * 19 * 53; the 8 divisors are {1, 2, 19, 38, 53, 106, 1007, 2014} formed by choosing subsets of {2, 19, 53}.

given.aim: Count the number of distinct positive integral factors of 2014.

terms.meaning: The year 2014 as an integer; our target number for prime factorisation
terms.connects: The number whose divisor count is sought

terms.meaning: The odd part of 2014, which factors as a product of two distinct primes
terms.connects: Completes the prime factorisation of 2014

terms.meaning: The number of positive divisors; since 2014 = 2^1 * 19^1 * 53^1, tau = (1+1)^3 = 8
terms.connects: The answer to the question

given.plan: Factorise 2014 completely into primes, then apply tau(n) = prod(e_i + 1) to count divisors.

steps.title: Prime factorisation of 2014
steps.formula_raw: every integer > 1 factors uniquely into a product of prime powers
steps.apply: 2014 / 2 = 1007. Is 1007 prime? 1007 / 7 is not integer; 1007 / 11 is not; 1007 / 13 is not; 1007 / 17 is not; 1007 / 19 = 53. Is 53 prime? Yes (not divisible by 2, 3, 5, 7 and sqrt(53) < 8). So 2014 = 2^1 * 19^1 * 53^1.
steps.note: All exponents are 1, so 2014 is squarefree.

solution.result: The number of distinct positive integral factors of 2014 is 8.

questions.formula_note: Since 2014 = 2 * 19 * 53 is squarefree with three prime factors, tau(2014) = 2^3 = 8; the divisor count formula is the key.

questions.question: The number of divisors of 2100 is ____.
questions.to_find: The total number of positive divisors of 2100

understand.plain: Factorise 2100 into prime powers, then use the multiplicative formula tau(n) = prod(e_i + 1). We get 2100 = 2^2 * 3 * 5^2 * 7, so tau(2100) = (2+1)(1+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 3 * 2 * 3 * 2 = 36. Each divisor independently chooses exponents from 0 to e_i for each prime.

keywords.explain: With four distinct primes 2, 3, 5, 7 and exponents 2, 1, 2, 1, the count is (2+1)(1+1)(2+1)(1+1).

understand.visual_alt: 2100 = 2^2 * 3 * 5^2 * 7; each prime independently contributes a (e_i+1) factor to the divisor count giving 3*2*3*2 = 36.

terms.meaning: The target number; 2100 = 2^2 * 3^1 * 5^2 * 7^1
terms.connects: The number to be factorised

terms.meaning: The exponents of primes 2, 3, 5, 7 in 2100's factorisation
terms.connects: Determine the divisor count via multiplication

terms.meaning: The number of positive divisors of 2100
terms.connects: The final answer

given.plan: Factorise 2100 into prime powers, then multiply (e_i + 1) across all prime factors to get tau(2100).

steps.title: Apply divisor count formula
steps.note: The answer 36 is consistent with 2100 having a moderately rich factorisation with four distinct prime factors.

solution.result: The number of divisors of 2100 is 36.

questions.formula_note: The divisor count formula tau(n) = prod(e_i + 1) over all prime-power factors is the essential tool; for 2100 = 2^2 * 3 * 5^2 * 7 it gives 3 * 2 * 3 * 2 = 36.

questions.question:
Let n = p^2 * q where p and q are distinct prime numbers. How many numbers m satisfy 1 <= m <= n and gcd(m, n) = 1? Note that gcd(m, n) is the greatest common divisor of m and n.
A. p(q-1)
B. pq
C. (p^2-1)(q-1)
D. p(p-1)(q-1)
questions.to_find: The number of integers m in [1, n] that are coprime to n = p^2 * q

understand.plain: The count of integers from 1 to n that are coprime to n is Euler's totient function phi(n). For n = p^2 * q (p and q distinct primes), phi(n) = phi(p^2) * phi(q) = p^2(1 - 1/p) * q(1 - 1/q) = p(p-1)(q-1). This counts m values with no shared prime factor with p^2 * q.

keywords.explain: phi(n) counts integers in {1, 2, ..., n} coprime to n. It is multiplicative: phi(ab) = phi(a) * phi(b) when gcd(a,b) = 1.
phi(p^k) = p^k - p^(k-1) = p^(k-1)(p-1). Integers not coprime to p^k are multiples of p: there are p^(k-1) of them.
For coprime a and b, phi(ab) = phi(a)*phi(b). Since gcd(p^2, q) = 1 for distinct primes, phi(p^2 * q) = phi(p^2) * phi(q).

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing multiples of p and multiples of q within {1, ..., n}; the count outside both circles is phi(n) = p(p-1)(q-1).

given.aim: Compute phi(n) = |{m : 1 <= m <= n, gcd(m,n) = 1}| for n = p^2 * q where p and q are distinct primes.

terms.meaning: The modulus; a product of a prime square and a distinct prime
terms.connects: The number whose totient we compute

terms.meaning: Totient of the prime-square factor: of p^2 numbers, those divisible by p are p^2/p = p, so phi(p^2) = p^2 - p
terms.connects: One factor in the multiplicative formula

terms.meaning: Totient of a prime q: all integers in {1, ..., q} except q itself are coprime to q
terms.connects: The other factor in the formula

given.plan: Use the multiplicativity of Euler's totient: phi(p^2 * q) = phi(p^2) * phi(q) = p(p-1) * (q-1) = p(p-1)(q-1).

steps.title: Factor n and use multiplicativity of phi
steps.formula_raw: phi(n) = phi(p_1^(e_1)) * phi(p_2^(e_2)) * ... when the p_i are distinct primes
steps.apply: n = p^2 * q with gcd(p^2, q) = 1 (p and q distinct primes). So phi(n) = phi(p^2) * phi(q).
steps.note: Multiplicativity requires the factors to be coprime, which is guaranteed since p != q are both prime.

steps.title: Compute phi(p^2) and phi(q), then multiply
steps.apply: phi(p^2) = p^(2-1)(p-1) = p(p-1). phi(q) = q - 1. Therefore phi(n) = p(p-1) * (q-1) = p(p-1)(q-1). This matches option D.
steps.note: Option A p(q-1) forgets the (p-1) factor from phi(p^2); option C (p^2-1)(q-1) uses phi(p^2) = p^2-1 which is wrong (that would be for phi(p^2) with exponent 1 in a different sense).

solution.result: The number of m with 1 <= m <= n and gcd(m, n) = 1 is p(p-1)(q-1), which is option D.

questions.formula_note: Euler's totient phi(n) = n * prod_{p|n} (1 - 1/p) and its multiplicative property are the key: for n = p^2 * q, phi = p(p-1)(q-1) (option D).

questions.question:
The minimum positive integer p such that 3^p (mod 17) = 1 is
A. 5
B. 8
C. 12
D. 16

understand.plain: We want the smallest positive p such that 3^p ≡ 1 (mod 17). This p is called the multiplicative order of 3 modulo 17. By Fermat's Little Theorem, 3^16 ≡ 1 (mod 17) since 17 is prime and gcd(3, 17) = 1. The order must divide 16; we check if a smaller divisor of 16 (1, 2, 4, 8) works. Since 3 is a primitive root mod 17, its order is exactly 16.

keywords.explain: The order of a mod p is the smallest positive k with a^k ≡ 1 (mod p). It must divide p-1 by Fermat's Little Theorem.
If p is prime and p does not divide a, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). So the order divides p-1.
An integer g whose order mod p equals p-1; such g generates all non-zero residues. 3 is a primitive root mod 17.
keywords.example: Powers of 3 mod 17: 3, 9, 10, 13, 5, 15, 11, 16, 14, 8, 7, 4, 12, 2, 6, 1 — all 16 non-zero residues appear.

understand.visual_alt: Number line of powers of 3 mod 17 cycling through all 16 non-zero residues before returning to 1, confirming the order is 16.

given.aim: Find the multiplicative order of 3 modulo 17.

terms.meaning: The remainder when 3^p is divided by 17; we seek the smallest p giving remainder 1
terms.connects: Defines the order as the period of this sequence

terms.meaning: The possible values for the order: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 (divisors of p-1 = 16)
terms.example: Order must divide 16 by Fermat; check 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8 (mod 17) for whether any equal 1
terms.connects: Narrows the search for the minimal p

terms.example: Since 3^8 ≡ -1 (mod 17), the order cannot be 1, 2, 4, or 8; it must be 16
terms.connects: Proves the order is exactly 16

given.plan: Use Fermat's Little Theorem to bound the order at 16; then check each divisor of 16 (1, 2, 4, 8) to show none gives 3^d ≡ 1 (mod 17), concluding the order is 16.

steps.title: Bound the order using Fermat's Little Theorem
steps.formula_raw: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p) for prime p and gcd(a,p) = 1
steps.apply: With a = 3, p = 17: 3^16 ≡ 1 (mod 17). The order of 3 mod 17 must divide 16. Divisors of 16 are 1, 2, 4, 8, 16.
steps.note: The order divides p-1 = 16; we just need to find the smallest divisor d such that 3^d ≡ 1 (mod 17).

steps.title: Check smaller divisors of 16
steps.note: 3^8 ≡ -1 (mod 17) is a useful shortcut: if 3^8 ≡ -1 then 3^(16) ≡ 1 but no smaller power of 2 works as the order.

solution.result: The minimum positive integer p such that 3^p ≡ 1 (mod 17) is 16 (option D).

questions.formula_note: Fermat's Little Theorem gives 3^16 ≡ 1 (mod 17); checking that no smaller divisor of 16 works (particularly 3^8 ≡ -1) confirms 3 is a primitive root mod 17, so the order is exactly 16.

questions.question:
The exponent of 11 in the prime factorization of 300! is
A. 27
B. 28
C. 29
D. 30
questions.to_find: The exponent of 11 in the prime factorization of 300!

understand.plain: Legendre's formula gives the exact exponent of a prime p in n! as the sum floor(n/p) + floor(n/p^2) + floor(n/p^3) + ... (until the terms become 0). For p = 11 and n = 300: floor(300/11) + floor(300/121) + floor(300/1331) = 27 + 2 + 0 = 29.

keywords.explain: The exponent of prime p in n! is sum_{k=1}^{infty} floor(n/p^k). It counts how many multiples of p, p^2, p^3, ... are in {1, ..., n}.
keywords.example: Exponent of 2 in 8! = floor(8/2) + floor(8/4) + floor(8/8) = 4+2+1 = 7.
Each multiple of 11 contributes at least one factor of 11 to 300!.

keywords.explain: 121 = 11^2. Multiples of 121 in {1, ..., 300}: 121 and 242 (since 3*121=363 > 300). These contribute an extra factor of 11.
keywords.example: 121! contains 11^2 in its factorisation from the factors 121 and 242.

understand.visual_alt: Three-tier counting: 27 multiples of 11, plus 2 multiples of 121, plus 0 multiples of 1331 in the range [1, 300], summing to 29.

given.aim: Find the exact power of 11 in the prime factorization of 300!.

terms.meaning: The product 1 * 2 * 3 * ... * 300; contains every integer from 1 to 300 as a factor
terms.example: 300! is an astronomically large number but we only need the exponent of 11 in it
terms.connects: The number whose prime factorisation exponent we compute

terms.meaning: Number of multiples of 11^k in {1, ..., 300}; each contributes k factors of 11
terms.connects: Terms in Legendre's sum

terms.meaning: The total exponent = sum of floor(300/11^k) for k = 1, 2, 3, ...
terms.connects: The formula that gives the exact answer

given.plan: Apply Legendre's formula: exponent of 11 in 300! = floor(300/11) + floor(300/121) + floor(300/1331) + ... until terms vanish.

steps.title: Count multiples of 11 in {1, ..., 300}
steps.apply: floor(300 / 11) = floor(27.27...) = 27. These are 11, 22, 33, ..., 297; there are 27 of them.
steps.note: Each multiple of 11 contributes at least one factor of 11 to 300!.

steps.title: Count multiples of 11^2 = 121 and higher powers
steps.apply: floor(300 / 121) = floor(2.479...) = 2 (the multiples 121 and 242 each contribute one extra factor of 11). floor(300 / 1331) = floor(0.225...) = 0. Higher powers also give 0. Total exponent = 27 + 2 + 0 = 29 (option C).
steps.note: 11^3 = 1331 > 300 so only the k=1 and k=2 terms contribute; the answer 29 eliminates option D (30) and option A (27 ignores 121-multiples).

solution.result: The exponent of 11 in the prime factorization of 300! is 27 + 2 = 29 (option C).

questions.formula_note: Legendre's formula v_p(n!) = sum_{k>=1} floor(n/p^k) is the essential tool; for p=11, n=300, only the first two terms (27 and 2) are nonzero, giving 29.

formulas.name: Euler's Totient Function (Multiplicative Form)
formulas.plain: phi(n) = prod_{p|n} p^(e_p - 1) * (p-1); equivalently phi(n) = n * prod_{p|n} (1 - 1/p)
formulas.whenToUse: Count integers from 1 to n coprime to n; appears in RSA, primitive roots, and order-of-elements problems

terms.means: number of integers in {1,...,n} coprime to n
exponent of prime p in factorisation of n

formulas.trap: phi is NOT simply n-1; for prime powers phi(p^k) = p^(k-1)(p-1), not p^k - 1.

formulas.name: Legendre's Formula (Prime Power in Factorial)
formulas.whenToUse: Find the exponent of a prime p in n!; count trailing zeros; determine when n! is divisible by p^k

terms.means: exponent of prime p in the prime factorisation of n!
number of multiples of p^k in {1, 2, ..., n}
the prime whose exponent is sought

formulas.trap: Do not just compute floor(n/p); you must add floor(n/p^2), floor(n/p^3), ... for multiples that contribute extra factors.
formulas.reference: Legendre, 1830; Number Theory: p-adic valuation of factorial
